findings.title: Pillar Two GloBE Information Return 30 June 2026 filing deadline — 8 days away
findings.summary: Multinational enterprise groups with calendar fiscal years face a 30 June 2026 deadline to file the first-ever 2024 GloBE Information Return (GIR)—18 months after fiscal year end under transitional rules. MNEs that timely file centrally in a qualifying jurisdiction are relieved from local filing in other adopting jurisdictions; however, four countries (Bahamas, North Macedonia, Slovak Republic, Vietnam) have not joined the OECD common understanding, meaning local filing remains required there regardless of any central filing arrangement.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who own or control multinational enterprise groups with consolidated revenues above EUR 750 million face an 8-day deadline to file or confirm central filing of the inaugural 2024 GloBE Information Return; clients with entities in non-participating jurisdictions (Bahamas, Slovak Republic, Vietnam, North Macedonia) require separate local filings regardless of any central filing arrangement.
findings.urgency_reason: The 30 June 2026 deadline is 8 days away; this is the first-ever GIR filing with no further OECD-level extension, and missed filings may trigger local penalties across multiple jurisdictions.

findings.title: Luxembourg EU public Country-by-Country Reporting: first filings due June 2026
findings.summary: Multinational groups with consolidated revenues exceeding EUR 750 million in each of the two preceding fiscal years must publicly report country-by-country tax data under Luxembourg's CbCR law implementing EU Directive 2021/2101. For entities with fiscal years beginning on or after 22 June 2024, the first reporting deadline falls in June 2026. Reports must be filed with the Luxembourg RCS and made publicly available.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients connected to multinational groups with EUR 750M+ revenues whose fiscal year began between 22 June and 31 December 2024 face a June 2026 CbCR filing deadline with the Luxembourg RCS; public disclosure of the report creates both legal exposure for non-compliance and reputational risk for groups whose tax positions become publicly visible.
findings.urgency_reason: For fiscal year companies with a year ending in mid-2025, the 12-month filing window closes in June 2026; groups on calendar fiscal years have until December 2026, but those on non-calendar fiscal years beginning after 22 June 2024 may be at or past deadline now.

findings.title: MAS revised Single Family Office framework effective 15 June 2026
findings.summary: The MAS revised framework for Single Family Offices took effect on 15 June 2026, granting a structure-agnostic class exemption from licensing. Qualifying SFOs need only notify MAS of operations, maintain a qualifying bank account, and file a simple annual return disclosing total AUM and bank name. Existing SFOs have a one-year transition period until 15 June 2027. 'Family member' is now defined as all lineal descendants of a common ancestor within five generations.
findings.client_impact: HNW families operating or considering a Singapore Single Family Office benefit from reduced regulatory burden under the new notification-and-annual-return regime, but existing structures must be reviewed for eligibility and transition filings must be completed before the 15 June 2027 deadline.
findings.urgency_reason: Framework is in effect now with a one-year transition period; clients need to engage advisers to assess eligibility and prepare notifications, but no deadline falls within the 30-day URGENT window.

findings.title: IoM FSA updates Beneficial Ownership FAQs: new SMO disclosure requirement and 15 September 2026 deadline
findings.summary: Following secondary legislation effective 25 May 2026, IoM entities where no individual meets the revised registrable beneficial owner threshold must now identify and report a Senior Managing Official to the Central Registry. A parallel consultation on 'legitimate interest' access to the register ran until 30 June 2026. Nominated officers must adopt the new process by 15 September 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Isle of Man entities for succession or asset-holding structures should verify whether changes to the beneficial owner ownership threshold affect who must be disclosed; if no individual meets the threshold, a Senior Managing Official must now be reported, expanding the disclosure footprint of IoM structures.
findings.urgency_reason: The 15 September 2026 deadline for nominated officers is approximately 85 days away; clients need to instruct their IoM corporate service providers now to review and update beneficial ownership records under the revised rules.
findings.source_name: Isle of Man Financial Services Authority (IOMFSA)

findings.title: Cyprus Pillar Two filing grace period: submit 2024 GloBE returns by 30 September 2026 without penalty
findings.summary: On 15 June 2026, the Cyprus Tax Department confirmed no penalties, interest, or charges will apply for late submission of 2024 GloBE returns and notifications—including top-up tax payment—provided filings are submitted by 30 September 2026, extending beyond the standard 30 June 2026 OECD GIR deadline.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Cyprus constituent entities in qualifying MNE groups have until 30 September 2026 to file 2024 GloBE returns without penalty exposure in Cyprus; advisers should confirm whether the grace period applies to their specific structure and begin preparing filings now to meet the extended deadline.
findings.urgency_reason: The 30 September 2026 deadline is approximately 100 days away, but preparation requires structural analysis of GloBE obligations; delay risks missing the grace period and triggering full penalty exposure.

findings.title: Cyprus 2026 tax reform: corporate rate raised to 15%, deemed dividend distribution abolished — restructuring review overdue
findings.summary: Effective 1 January 2026, Cyprus raised its corporate income tax rate to 15% (from 12.5%), abolished deemed dividend distribution (SDIT), revised capital gains exemptions, raised personal income tax thresholds, and tightened reporting and enforcement rules, aligning with OECD Pillar Two.
findings.client_impact: The rate increase to 15% and abolition of SDIT fundamentally alter the tax efficiency of Cyprus holding, IP-holding, and profit-repatriation structures; HNW clients with existing Cyprus structures should review whether their arrangements remain optimal or require restructuring under the new permanent framework.
findings.urgency_reason: Already in effect since 1 January 2026; any deferred post-enactment restructuring review is now overdue, and new distributions or transactions conducted under pre-reform assumptions may produce unexpected tax results.

findings.title: Switzerland FINMA revised AMLO-FINMA: tighter beneficial ownership and indirect control chain documentation incoming
findings.summary: FINMA's consultation on the partially revised Anti-Money Laundering Ordinance (AMLO-FINMA) closed 9 June 2026. Key proposed changes include reinforced requirements to understand client structure, tightened requirements for payable-through accounts and sub-accounts, and strengthened documentation of indirect control chains for beneficial ownership identification, complementing Switzerland's broader LETA/AMLA reform package.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients banking in Switzerland with complex multi-layered holding structures, nominee arrangements, or sub-account arrangements should expect enhanced documentation requests and more detailed UBO control chain analysis from Swiss financial intermediaries once the revised AMLO-FINMA is finalised and takes effect.
findings.urgency_reason: Consultation just closed; final rules are expected in late 2026 as part of the LETA/AMLA reform package — clients with Swiss banking relationships should begin preparing documentation now to avoid account-opening and KYC-refresh friction.

findings.title: Switzerland LETA federal beneficial ownership register expected in force autumn 2026; 500,000+ entities affected
findings.summary: The Federal Act on the Transparency of Legal Entities (LETA), passed 26 September 2025, creates a non-public federal UBO register covering Swiss GmbHs, AGs, cooperatives, and foreign entities with effective management in Switzerland, Swiss real estate, or a Swiss branch. Over 500,000 entities must file within one month of LETA taking effect; fines up to CHF 500,000 apply for non-compliance.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Swiss corporate entities or foreign entities that own Swiss real estate or are managed from Switzerland must prepare UBO disclosure filings; the non-public register restricts access to designated authorities but nonetheless creates a formal transparency record that significantly reduces anonymity in Swiss structures.
findings.urgency_reason: Entry into force expected in approximately 3-4 months; clients need to identify their Swiss UBO obligations, gather documentation, and brief their Swiss corporate administrators now to meet the one-month registration window after the law takes effect.

findings.title: UAE new AML/CFT law in effect October 2025; FATF 5th Round onsite evaluation underway June 2026
findings.summary: The UAE's new AML and CFT law (effective 14 October 2025) introduces standalone proliferation financing offences, expands predicate offences to include tax evasion, and explicitly covers digital assets and virtual asset service providers. The FATF 5th Round onsite visit is underway in June 2026—the first evaluation since the UAE exited the grey list in February 2024—creating heightened compliance scrutiny across UAE financial institutions.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with UAE-based structures, family office arrangements, or digital asset holdings face heightened due diligence scrutiny from UAE financial institutions during and after the FATF evaluation; the expansion of predicate offences to include tax evasion means UAE advisers will scrutinise international tax planning arrangements with greater care.
findings.urgency_reason: The FATF evaluation is live in June 2026, creating immediate compliance pressure on UAE financial institutions; clients with any UAE structure review or onboarding pending should expect heightened scrutiny right now.
findings.source_name: First Compliance Solution / Herbert Smith Freehills Kramer

findings.title: UAE UBO: 15-day change reporting requirement with AED 50,000+ fines; heightened FATF enforcement
findings.summary: Under Cabinet Decision No. 109 of 2023, UAE companies must report changes in shareholding, control rights, or beneficial owner identity to the licensing authority within 15 days. Fines start at AED 50,000 for non-disclosure, with progressive penalties for repeat violations. Compliance is under heightened scrutiny ahead of the June 2026 FATF evaluation.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with active UAE corporate portfolios—including free zone and mainland companies—must ensure any recent changes in ownership structure have been reported within the 15-day window; non-compliance identified during the FATF evaluation period could trigger immediate enforcement action.
findings.urgency_reason: The FATF evaluation underway in June 2026 places UAE licensing authorities on high alert; even historical lapses in UBO updating may be scrutinised, making prompt compliance review particularly important right now.

findings.title: Luxembourg carried interest tax reform: broadened eligibility and permanent preferential regime from 2026 tax year
findings.summary: Draft Law No. 8590 revamps Luxembourg's carried interest tax regime for alternative investment fund managers from the 2026 tax year, broadening the category of eligible beneficiaries, clarifying covered arrangements, and creating a permanent preferential tax regime designed to compete with comparable UK and French frameworks.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who are managers or participants in Luxembourg-domiciled alternative investment funds with carried interest arrangements should review whether their structures qualify under the broadened regime and consider whether restructuring can optimise tax treatment before year-end crystallization events.
findings.urgency_reason: Applicable from the 2026 tax year; fund managers and investors with carried interest arrangements approaching vesting or exit events this year should seek Luxembourg tax advice to confirm eligibility and optimise outcomes before December 2026.

findings.title: FinCEN interim final rule: all US domestic companies fully exempt from BOI reporting under Corporate Transparency Act
findings.summary: FinCEN's interim final rule, effective February 2026, removes BOI reporting obligations for all US-incorporated entities and US persons under the Corporate Transparency Act. Foreign entities that meet the reporting company definition must still report, but are not required to identify US persons as beneficial owners. The Eleventh Circuit has affirmed the CTA's constitutionality.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with US domestic LLCs, corporations, and limited partnerships are now fully exempt from CTA BOI filing requirements—a significant reversal that reduces domestic compliance burden; clients with foreign-incorporated entities registered in the US must still assess their reporting company status and file accordingly.
findings.urgency_reason: Rule is in effect since February 2026; clients who had been preparing or had completed US entity BOI filings should confirm exemption status, and advisers should update compliance guidance to reflect the bifurcated treatment of domestic versus foreign-incorporated entities.

findings.title: Florida SB 264: foreign buyer restrictions upheld by courts; mandatory affidavit requirement at every closing remains in force
findings.summary: Florida SB 264 (effective July 2023), restricting real estate purchases by nationals of China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, and Syria, was upheld by courts in November 2025. Chinese nationals face a near-total ban on any Florida real estate; nationals of other listed countries are prohibited within 10 miles of military installations or critical infrastructure. All Florida real estate closings require a perjury-backed affidavit from the buyer.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients from any of the seven listed countries must be screened before any Florida property transaction is initiated; Chinese national clients face a near-total prohibition on Florida real estate, and all other buyers must provide a sworn affidavit at closing — failure to screen creates legal exposure for both buyer and any advisers facilitating the transaction.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is upheld and fully in force; this is an ongoing compliance requirement for every Florida real estate transaction involving foreign buyers — advisers must apply the nationality screening process to every transaction without exception.

findings.title: Brazil BCB Resolution 561: stablecoin/crypto settlement banned for overseas payments effective 1 October 2026
findings.summary: BCB Resolution 561 bans fintechs and payment providers from settling overseas payments in stablecoins or crypto, effective 1 October 2026. Stablecoins currently account for approximately 90% of Brazil's cross-border crypto volume. The ban will force reversion to traditional correspondent banking with higher costs and multi-day settlement windows.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients and their businesses that use stablecoin rails for Brazil cross-border payments—including international remittances, asset purchases, and operational payments—must transition to traditional SWIFT-based correspondent banking before 1 October 2026, accepting higher transaction costs and longer settlement timelines.
findings.urgency_reason: The 1 October 2026 deadline is approximately 100 days away; clients relying on stablecoin settlement for regular Brazil payment flows need to engage banking partners and restructure payment arrangements now to avoid disruption at the ban date.

findings.title: Malta: private trustees required to file beneficial ownership for administered trusts from January 2026
findings.summary: Following Legal Notice 127 of 2025, Malta now requires private trustees to submit beneficial ownership information for administered trusts to the Companies Registry. Initial data filing was due 11 January 2026; annual confirmations and timely updates are required thereafter. The broader EU Anti-Money Laundering Regulation (AMLR) will harmonise these requirements across the EU from 10 July 2027.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who have established Malta-administered trusts—including succession or asset-protection structures—should confirm that their Malta-licensed trustee has made the required BOI disclosure; beneficial owners, settlors, protectors, and discretionary beneficiaries are now on record with Maltese authorities, with implications for confidentiality and future AML reviews.
findings.urgency_reason: Initial filing was due January 2026 and may have been missed; annual confirmation cycles are now active, and any late or incomplete filings should be rectified promptly to avoid regulatory penalties for the trustee.

findings.title: DAC8/CARF in force from January 2026: crypto-asset transactions now subject to automatic tax information exchange
findings.summary: EU Directive DAC8 (incorporating the CARF crypto-asset reporting framework) applied from 1 January 2026, extending automatic exchange of information to digital assets and closing the transparency gap between traditional and digital asset holdings. Separately, HMRC announced discontinuation of its combined CRS/FATCA XML schema after 31 December 2026, requiring UK financial institutions to use separate schemas from January 2027.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with digital asset holdings, crypto exchange accounts, or tokenised securities on platforms serving EU users are now subject to automatic exchange of information with tax authorities in their home jurisdictions; this closes the transparency gap that previously allowed offshore crypto holdings to escape tax reporting, and clients with undisclosed digital assets face material detection risk.
findings.urgency_reason: DAC8/CARF reporting is active for the 2026 tax year; clients with unreported digital asset holdings should seek voluntary disclosure advice before year-end exchange cycles expose positions to tax authority scrutiny.

findings.title: EU AMLA: common SAR template and group-wide AML/CFT policy standards due by 10 July 2026
findings.summary: The EU's Anti-Money Laundering Authority (AMLA) must deliver by 10 July 2026: implementing technical standards for a common suspicious activity report (SAR) template, draft regulatory technical standards for group-wide AML/CFT policies, and delegated acts on sanctions requirements. AMLA will directly supervise 40 large high-risk EU financial institutions from July 2027.
findings.client_impact: Private banks and wealth management firms operating across EU jurisdictions (Luxembourg, Malta, Cyprus) will receive standardised SAR reporting and group policy requirements following the July 2026 publication; HNW clients served by these institutions can expect more uniform and potentially more intensive due diligence and transaction monitoring as standards harmonise practice across member states.
findings.urgency_reason: AMLA's publication deadline is 18 days away; once standards are published, EU-regulated financial institutions will adapt compliance infrastructure on an accelerated timeline, likely triggering near-term KYC refresh requests for clients of EU private banks.

findings.title: Bahamas excluded from Pillar Two GIR central filing common understanding — separate local filing required
findings.summary: As of 12 May 2026, the Bahamas is among four jurisdictions (with North Macedonia, Slovak Republic, and Vietnam) that have not joined the OECD common understanding on GIR central filing. MNE groups with entities in the Bahamas cannot satisfy Bahamas local filing obligations through central filing elsewhere; a separate 2024 GIR must be filed with Bahamas tax authorities by the applicable local deadline.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients whose MNE groups include Bahamas entities—such as Bahamas SICAVs, foundations, or holding companies—face a separate local GIR filing obligation that cannot be discharged by central filing in Luxembourg, Ireland, or another qualifying jurisdiction.
findings.urgency_reason: Directly linked to the 30 June 2026 URGENT GIR deadline above; clients with Bahamas entities should immediately confirm whether a local Bahamas Pillar Two filing is required and, if so, the status of that filing.

findings.title: MAS Circular AMLD 05/2026: risk-proportionate Source of Wealth procedures to streamline Singapore private banking account opening
findings.summary: MAS Circular AMLD 05/2026 (May 2026) directs financial institutions to apply risk-proportionate SOW verification, targeting excessive process at lower-risk clients. MAS expects the median private banking account opening time to fall below one month. Thematic reviews and inspections from Q1 2026 are assessing adherence.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients opening Singapore private banking accounts should experience faster onboarding where risk profiles are straightforward; higher-risk clients or those with complex international structures will continue to face intensive SOW scrutiny, which should become more precisely calibrated to actual risk indicators.
findings.urgency_reason: Operational guidance with no new client filing deadline; relevant background for clients with Singapore private banking relationships or those considering opening accounts.

findings.title: BVI legitimate interest access to beneficial ownership register launched 1 April 2026
findings.summary: From 1 April 2026, the BVI Registry accepts applications from persons demonstrating 'legitimate interest' for case-by-case access to beneficial ownership information, initially targeting obliged entities such as banks. Access is restricted to 25%+ ownership-interest holders or equivalent control; the register remains non-public with strict disclosure limitations and exemptions for vulnerable individuals.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using BVI companies and limited partnerships should be aware that financial institutions conducting AML due diligence on counterparties or investees can now request BVI ownership information through the VIRRGIN system; confidentiality of BVI structures has diminished incrementally relative to prior years, though direct public access is not available.
findings.urgency_reason: Access regime is in operation since April 2026; no immediate client action required, but relevant background for clients who have relied on BVI confidentiality as a structural feature of their holdings.

findings.title: Cayman Islands tokenised mutual and private fund framework in force 24 March 2026
findings.summary: The Mutual Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 and Private Funds (Amendment) Act, 2026 establish clear rules for tokenised funds using distributed ledger technology to record ownership interests, with CIMA oversight and investor protections built into the framework.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients exploring tokenised fund structures—whether as investors or sponsors—can now use Cayman-regulated frameworks with clear legal treatment of DLT-based ownership interests, providing a regulated pathway for digital asset fund investments and allowing existing managers to offer tokenised feeder or parallel structures.
findings.urgency_reason: New framework available since March 2026; no compliance deadline for existing conventional fund investors, but relevant for clients considering or being offered tokenised fund investments.

findings.title: CIMA 2026 fee increases: registered fund annual fee CI$4,125; FAR filing fee up 28%
findings.summary: Effective 1 January 2026, CIMA increased annual registered fund fees to CI$4,125 (approx. USD 5,030) and master fund fees to CI$3,075 (approx. USD 3,750). The Fund Annual Return filing fee increased approximately 28% to CI$450 per fund per year. Changes apply to both Mutual Funds and Private Funds registered with CIMA.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who sponsor or hold interests in Cayman registered funds will see modestly higher fund operating expenses from 2026; clients with multiple Cayman fund vehicles should budget for the cumulative impact across their portfolio.
findings.urgency_reason: Already in effect since January 2026; purely informational for clients reviewing fund cost structures or setting fund administration budgets.

findings.title: IRS mandating electronic FIRPTA withholding payments via EFTPS; 15% rate unchanged
findings.summary: The IRS is requiring all FIRPTA withholding payments to be submitted electronically via EFTPS. The 15% withholding rate on foreign persons' dispositions of US real property interests remains unchanged; the reduced 10% rate for residential property sold for USD 1 million or less used as a residence also remains available.
findings.client_impact: Foreign HNW clients disposing of US real estate interests should confirm their withholding agent is set up for EFTPS electronic submission; no change to withholding rates means the tax economics of US real estate dispositions are unaffected.
findings.urgency_reason: Procedural change only with no impact on rates or substantive FIRPTA obligations; relevant for transactions currently in process or where a foreign seller is approaching a US real estate closing.

findings.title: Jersey and Guernsey consulting on legitimate interest access to central beneficial ownership registers
findings.summary: Both Jersey (consultation closed January 2026) and Guernsey (consultation closed April 2026) are consulting on case-by-case restricted access to their central UBO registers for AML/CFT purposes. Access would be through a secure application process with strict disclosure limitations, exemptions for vulnerable individuals, and controls on data use.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Jersey or Guernsey trusts, companies, or limited partnerships should monitor the outcome of both consultations; if access frameworks are adopted, financial institutions conducting AML due diligence will be able to request UBO information from these registers, incrementally reducing Channel Islands confidentiality.
findings.urgency_reason: Both consultations are closed and outcomes are pending; no immediate client action required, but framework changes implementing restricted access may follow within 6-12 months.

findings.title: Panama UBO registry (RUB) mandatory with no ownership threshold; bearer shares must be immobilized
findings.summary: All Panamanian legal entities must register their ultimate beneficial owners with the Unique Registry of Beneficial Owners (RUB) with no ownership percentage threshold exemption. Bearer shares must be immobilized with a licensed custodian. Annual financial reporting is due by 30 April each year, and companies must report the physical address of accounting records to their Resident Agent.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using Panama corporations or foundations for asset holding or succession planning should confirm with their resident agent that RUB registration is current, all beneficial owners are disclosed, and any bearer shares have been properly immobilized with a licensed custodian.
findings.urgency_reason: Ongoing compliance regime with no new immediate deadline; a periodic compliance reminder for clients with Panamanian entities whose beneficial ownership data may not have been updated to reflect recent structural changes.

findings.title: Brazil VASP authorization deadline 30 October 2026; capital requirements transition due 30 June 2026
findings.summary: VASPs operating in Brazil must comply with new capital methodology rules under BCB Resolutions 519-521 by 30 June 2026 and apply for formal BCB authorization by 30 October 2026. Minimum capital requirements range from USD 181,500 to USD 544,500 depending on activity, with gradual step-up requirements through 2027.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who operate or hold controlling interests in Brazilian digital asset exchanges or payment platforms must ensure capital methodology compliance immediately (deadline 30 June 2026) and prepare formal BCB authorization applications before October 2026; failure to obtain authorization will prevent continued legal VASP operations in Brazil.
findings.urgency_reason: Capital methodology compliance is due 30 June 2026 (8 days), but this applies specifically to clients with direct VASP operational exposure; passive investors in crypto assets are not directly affected.

findings.title: Bahamas advances compliance legislation ahead of FATF 5th Round Mutual Evaluation
findings.summary: The Bahamas has updated six laws, including the Register of Beneficial Ownership Act, to strengthen compliance and international cooperation ahead of its FATF 5th Round Mutual Evaluation. The UBO database remains non-public. The Bahamas had achieved '40 for 40' compliance with FATF Recommendations prior to the evaluation.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using Bahamas entities, foundations, or investment funds should expect more rigorous KYC and beneficial ownership verification from Bahamas-licensed service providers as the jurisdiction prepares for heightened FATF scrutiny.
findings.urgency_reason: Background regulatory strengthening with no specific client filing deadline; relevant for clients with Bahamas structures who may experience enhanced due diligence from local service providers in the near term.

_discarded_findings_note: 1 finding discarded as outside FGC practice areas: Luxembourg MiFID II revised research unbundling rules (investment firm research payment obligations, no direct HNW client impact).
